_meta.note: ANONYMIZED, English, k>=3-org distilled patterns from Binclusive's dynamic-audit corpus. Failure-shapes clustered offline by LLM (frozen in data/clusters/), gated k>=3-distinct-org and anonymized by deterministic code. Contains NO customer identifiers (org/project ids, elements, urls stripped; frequency as tier only). Regenerate by re-running the distiller.

patterns.failureShape: A form input (text field, combobox, date picker, textarea) has no programmatically associated label — it relies on a placeholder or nearby visual text — so assistive tech announces it without a name or with the wrong name.
patterns.fix: Associate every input with a real label: a <label for> matching the input id, or aria-label / aria-labelledby. Never rely on placeholder as the accessible name. For custom comboboxes, also link the visible label to the widget.

patterns.failureShape: The page's top-level heading structure is wrong: no H1, multiple H1s, the page starts at H2, or visually prominent headings are not marked up as headings at all — so screen-reader users get no reliable map of the page.
patterns.fix: Give each page exactly one H1 that names its main topic, mark every visual heading with a real heading tag, and reserve lower levels (H2–H6) for subsections. Don't use multiple H1s for sibling sections.

patterns.failureShape: Content that is visible on screen (article text, cards, list items, prices, navigation) is missing from the accessibility tree, so screen-reader users cannot reach or hear it — often because a wrapper carries aria-hidden="true", display is mismanaged on injected content, or dynamic content never renders into the tree.
patterns.fix: Make sure meaningful content is exposed to assistive tech: never put aria-hidden="true" on a container that holds real content, render route-changed/dynamically loaded content into the DOM and accessibility tree, and verify the main region is reachable in screen-reader browse mode. Manage focus to newly revealed content.

patterns.failureShape: Heading levels are non-sequential — the page jumps levels (e.g. H1 to H3, H2 to H6) or nests them out of order, so the heading outline misrepresents the document structure.
patterns.fix: Nest headings sequentially without skipping levels (H1 then H2 then H3…). Pick the level by the section's place in the outline, not by its visual size; use CSS for sizing.

patterns.failureShape: A visual list (navigation groups, benefits, cards, track lists) is built from generic elements instead of list markup, so screen-reader users don't hear it as a list or get an item count.
patterns.fix: Mark up lists with <ul>/<ol> and <li> so assistive tech announces the list and its length. Group each repeated item in its own <li>.

patterns.failureShape: The page lacks landmark regions (no <main>, <nav>, <header>, or <footer>), so screen-reader users can't jump to the primary content or navigate by region.
patterns.fix: Wrap major page sections in HTML5 landmarks: <main> for primary content, <nav> for navigation, <header>/<footer> (banner/contentinfo) for the page chrome. One main landmark per page.

patterns.failureShape: Tabular data (prices, rates, schedules, comparisons) is built from <div>s or plain text instead of a real table, or a table lacks header semantics, so screen-reader users can't tie a value to its row/column header.
patterns.fix: Mark up tabular data with a semantic <table>: <thead> with <th scope> header cells, <tbody> with <td> data cells, and a <caption> naming the table's purpose. Don't fake tables with divs.
